behaviors.description: Speech-to-text output preserves speaker labels so the summary can attribute statements to specific attendees
behaviors.justification: The core failure mode is that speaker labels from a real speech-to-text API are either not returned or dropped before reaching the summary. A mock STT service that always includes speaker labels would hide this real infrastructure failure. We must call a real STT API to verify speaker labels are actually available in the API response. We also need the LLM to verify attribution works (Agentic), but the minimum level to catch the label preservation failure is System (real API call to STT).
behaviors.test_description: Call a real speech-to-text API with a test meeting recording containing multiple distinct speakers. Verify the API response includes speaker identifiers (e.g., 'Speaker 1', 'Speaker A', or speaker names). Trace these labels through the service pipeline to the LLM input. Call a real LLM to generate the summary and verify that the output contains speaker attribution (e.g., 'Alice noted that...', 'John proposed...' with speaker names, not generic pronouns), confirming labels were preserved and used for disambiguation.

behaviors.description: Structured summary contains at least one action item for every attendee assigned a task during the meeting
behaviors.justification: The failure mode is that the real LLM misses or fails to capture some action items despite speaker labels and context being available. A mock LLM can be scripted to always output all assignments correctly, but a real LLM might miss assignments due to ambiguous language, context loss, or other reasoning failures. We need a real LLM processing a real meeting transcript with known task assignments to verify the LLM identifies all of them.
behaviors.test_description: Create a meeting transcript with multiple attendees (Alice, Bob, Carol, David) where each is explicitly assigned at least one task (e.g., 'Alice will draft the spec by Friday; Bob will provision infrastructure; Carol will coordinate QA; David will review security'). Call a real LLM to generate the structured summary. Extract the action_items array and verify that every attendee mentioned in the transcript who received a task has at least one action item entry with the correct assignee, with no assignments dropped or misattributed.

behaviors.description: Key decisions mentioned in the transcript appear in the 'decisions' section of the summary, not buried in the narrative
behaviors.justification: The failure mode is that a real LLM either fails to identify decisions mentioned in the meeting or places them in the wrong output section (narrative, notes, etc.) despite the schema explicitly defining a 'decisions' section. A mock LLM configured to always place decisions correctly would hide this real failure of understanding and structural discipline. Only a real LLM processing a real transcript with explicit decisions can verify this behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Provide a meeting transcript with multiple explicit decisions clearly stated (e.g., 'We've decided to use PostgreSQL instead of MongoDB', 'We will launch in Q2, not Q1', 'We are deprioritizing mobile support'). Call a real LLM to generate a structured summary with a schema that includes a dedicated 'decisions' field/section. Verify that all explicitly mentioned decisions appear in the decisions array/section, not in narrative or notes fields, and that the decisions are accurately captured.

behaviors.description: Audio longer than 60 minutes is split into segments, processed sequentially, and merged into a coherent summary
behaviors.justification: This behavior involves multiple sequential infrastructure components and failure modes. Real failures occur across stages: (a) audio splitting fails or loses data on real audio files (System), (b) segments are processed out of order (Integration), (c) context is lost during sequential processing causing incoherence (Agentic), (d) merged summary has conflicting facts or broken references across segment boundaries (requires real LLM to assess coherence). Testing requires real audio (>60 min), real STT API, sequential processing logic, and real LLM coherence assessment—all these steps in sequence constitutes a Workflow-level test.
behaviors.test_description: Obtain a real meeting recording longer than 60 minutes (e.g., 90-minute recorded meeting) or create a test recording by concatenating segments. Run the service and verify: (a) the service detects audio duration >60 min and splits it into segments (e.g., 3 segments of ~30 min each), (b) each segment is transcribed via real STT API, (c) segments are processed sequentially with transcripts passed to real LLM in order, (d) verify segment boundaries don't drop content (check for continuity markers, speaker transitions), (e) generate the merged summary using real LLM and assess coherence: contextual references in later segments correctly resolve to earlier segment content, no contradictory conclusions, consistent speaker identification and decision tracking across all segments.

behaviors.description: Full pipeline—upload audio, transcribe, summarize, deliver structured output—completes without manual intervention for a real meeting recording
behaviors.justification: This is an explicit end-to-end test of the complete service pipeline with all real infrastructure components. Real failures occur at multiple integration points: (a) audio upload fails (System), (b) STT API timeout or malformed response (System), (c) LLM generation fails or times out (Agentic), (d) output serialization breaks the expected schema (Integration), (e) entire workflow fails mid-stream requiring retry or intervention (Workflow). Testing this requires executing all stages with real infrastructure—real audio file, real STT API, real LLM, real network calls, and observing the complete flow without intervention.
behaviors.test_description: Execute a true end-to-end pipeline test with a real meeting recording (audio file >5 min): (a) upload the audio file through the service API, (b) verify the service automatically sends it to the speech-to-text API without user action, (c) verify the transcript is automatically routed to the real LLM for summarization, (d) verify the structured output (action items array, decisions section, speaker attributions) is returned correctly and in the expected JSON schema, (e) verify the service completes fully without requiring manual intervention, retry, or polling, and (f) monitor for graceful handling of potential transient failures (e.g., API rate limits, timeouts) by implementing retry logic or queuing without blocking the caller.
